audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate. OpenShift's Security Context Constraint feature is on by default in OpenShift 4 and applied to all pods deployed. SCC selection is determined by a combination of the values in the securityContext and the rolebindings for the account deploying the pod. Run the following command to obtain a list of pods that are using privileged security context constraints: oc get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations."openshift.io/scc"|test("privileged"?)) | .metadata.name' Review each pod to ensure it requires the use of privileged security context constraints, which is the most relaxed security context constraint available in OpenShift by default. Run the following command to obtain a list of pods that are not using security context constraints at all: oc get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations."openshift.io/scc" == null) | .metadata.name' Review each pod and determine if there is an existing security context constraint that it should be using.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers.
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
justification: The task of auditing security contexts in OpenShift can be effectively automated using the OpenShift CLI (`oc`) commands, which can be scripted to check for compliance across all pods in a cluster. The second AI opinion correctly identifies the potential for automation by integrating these checks into a CI/CD pipeline or using IBM Cloud's monitoring capabilities. This approach leverages existing CLI tools to automate the process, making it more efficient and less prone to human error.
automation_summary: - IBM Cloud Resource/Service: OpenShift on IBM Cloud - API/CLI Method: OpenShift CLI (`oc`) commands - Properties to Check: `.metadata.annotations."openshift.io/scc"`, `.spec.securityContext` - Pass Criteria: Security contexts are defined and do not use privileged constraints unless necessary - Fail Criteria: Pods without a security context or using privileged constraints without justification
default_value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods.

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources.
audit: In OpenShift, projects (namespaces) are used to group and isolate related objects. When a request is made to create a new project using the web console or oc new- project command, an endpoint in OpenShift Container Platform is used to provision the project according to a template, which can be customized. The cluster administrator can allow and configure how developers and service accounts can create, or self-provision, their own projects. Regular users do not have access to the default project. Projects starting with openshift- and kube- host cluster components that run as Pods and other infrastructure components. As such, OpenShift does not allow you to create Projects starting with openshift- or kube- using the oc new-project command. For more information, see Working with projects and Configuring project creation Run the following command to list all resources in the default namespace, besides the kubernetes and openshift services, which are expected to be in the default namespace: oc get all -n default -o json | jq '.items[] | select((.kind|test("Service")) and (.metadata.name|test("openshift|kubernetes"))? | not) | (.kind + "/" + .metadata.name)' Carefully review the list of returned resources and consider moving them to another namespace.
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace.
impact: None
justification: The task of verifying resources in the default namespace and ensuring they are not misconfigured can be efficiently automated using IBM Cloud CLI and OpenShift CLI commands. These tools can script the retrieval and analysis of namespace configurations, making the audit process more efficient and consistent. The second AI opinion correctly highlights the potential for automation, which aligns with the capabilities of IBM Cloud APIs.
automation_summary: - IBM Cloud Resource/Service: OpenShift on IBM Cloud - API/CLI Method: OpenShift CLI (`oc`) commands - Properties to Check: Resources in the default namespace, project creation restrictions - Pass Criteria: Only `openshift` and `kube` resources exist in the default namespace; project creation with restricted prefixes is not allowed. - Fail Criteria: Presence of non-`openshift`/`kube` resources in the default namespace; unrestricted project creation with `openshift-` or `kube-` prefixes.
default_value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used

title: Encrypt etc
description: ETCD is not enabled by default; enabling it can provide an additional layer of protection.
rationale: You can enable etcd encryption for your cluster to provide an additional layer of data security. For example, it can help protect the loss of sensitive data if an etcd backup is exposed to the incorrect parties.
audit: Execute oc get openshiftapiserver -o=jsonpath='{range .items[0].status.conditions[?(@.type=="Encrypted")]}{.reason}{"\n"}{.message} {"\n"}' The output should show EncryptionCompleted upon successful encryption: EncryptionCompleted All resources encrypted: routes.route.openshift.io, oauthaccesstokens.oauth.openshift.io, oauthauthorizetokens.oauth.openshift.io
remediation: 1. Modify the API Server Object oc edit apiserver 2. Set the encryption field type to aescbc: spec: encryption: type: aescbc 3. Save the file to apply the changes. The encryption process starts. It can take 20 minutes or longer for this process to complete, depending on the size of your cluster.
impact: With encrpytion on etcd the following contents are encrypted at rest Secrets Config maps Routes OAuth access tokens OAuth authorize tokens
justification: The control involves checking the encryption status of etcd in an OpenShift environment, which can be automated using CLI commands. Although the initial suggestion was to use the `oc` command, which is specific to OpenShift, this can be integrated into IBM Cloud's environment by leveraging IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS) CLI or APIs. This aligns with IBM Cloud's capabilities to manage and monitor Kubernetes clusters, making automation feasible.
automation_summary: - IBM Cloud Resource/Service: IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS) - API/CLI Method: Use IBM Cloud CLI or IKS CLI to execute the `oc get openshiftapiserver` command within the context of the cluster. - Properties to Check: The output should include `EncryptionCompleted` and the message "All resources encrypted" with references to `routes.route.openshift.io`, `oauthaccesstokens.oauth.openshift.io`, and `oauthauthorizetokens.oauth.openshift.io`. - Pass Criteria: The output contains `EncryptionCompleted` and lists all specified resources as encrypted. - Fail Criteria: The absence of `EncryptionCompleted` or missing any of the specified resources in the encryption message.
default_value: By default ETCD is not encrypted

title: Minimize access to privileged Security Context Constraints
description: OpenShift has the concept of Security Context Constraints (SCCs) that supplement the Pod Security Admission controller. SCCs allow you to group elevated container capabilities and assign those capabilities to users and groups. For example, you can have an SCC that restricts the ability to launch privileged containers and assign that SCC to all authenticated users. As a result, users requesting a pod that contains a privileged container will be rejected. You can find more information on SCCs in the OpenShift documentation .
rationale: SCCs that contain the ability to permit privileged or elevated container action should be carefully managed. Users with access to such an SCC can leverage the privileged functionality granted by that SCC, increasing the risk of compromising the container or host.
audit: Find all users and groups with access to SCCs that include privileged or elevated capabilities: oc get scc -ojson | jq '.items[]|select(.allowHostIPC or .allowHostPID or .allowHostPorts or .allowHostNetwork or .allowHostDirVolumePlugin or .allowPrivilegedContainer or .runAsUser.type != "MustRunAsRange" )|.metadata.name,{"Group:":.groups},{"User":.users}' Review the returned users and groups and verify they actually need access to those SCCs.
remediation: Remove any users and groups who do not need access to an SCC, following the principle of least privilege. You can remove users and groups from an SCC using the oc edit scc $NAME command. Additionally, you can create your own SCCs that contain the container functionality you need for a particular use case and assign that SCC to users and groups if the default SCCs are not appropriate for your use case.
impact: Users should only have access to SCCs that allow them to perform functions required by their roles, and no more, following the principle of least privilege.
justification: The compliance control requires a detailed review and understanding of the SCC configurations, which involves interpreting JSON outputs and making decisions about user and group access. While the retrieval of SCC configurations can be automated using IBM Cloud CLI commands, the critical aspect of this control is the human judgment needed to assess whether users or groups should have access to privileged SCCs. This decision-making process cannot be fully automated as it requires context-specific analysis.
manual_summary: 1. **Access OpenShift Cluster:** Open the IBM Cloud Console and navigate to the OpenShift cluster management section. 2. **Retrieve SCC Configurations:** Use the IBM Cloud CLI to list all Security Context Constraints by entering the command: ``` oc get scc -o json ``` 3. **Review SCCs:** Examine the output JSON data for SCCs that allow elevated privileges such as `allowPrivilegedContainer`, `allowHostIPC`, `allowHostPID`, etc. Identify users or groups listed under these SCCs. 4. **Assess Necessity:** Determine if the users or groups with access to these SCCs are necessary for their roles within the project. This involves understanding the specific requirements of each user or group. 5. **Adjust Access:** For any unnecessary users or groups, remove their access using: ``` oc edit scc $NAME ``` Ensure that only users requiring access to a specific SCC for their role have that access. 6. **Document Changes:** Record any changes made to SCC access to maintain an audit trail.
default_value: OpenShift provides the following SCCs by default: "anyuid" "hostaccess" "hostmount-anyuid" "hostnetwork" "hostnetwork-v2" "machine-api-termination-handler" "node-exporter" "nonroot" "nonroot-v2" "privileged" "restricted" "restricted-v2" These default SCCs attempt to group similar privileged container functionality into a single SCC that fits particular use cases. Please refer to the OpenShift documentation for a complete list of capabilities associated with each default SCC.

title: Restrict GPU and USB pass through to approved devices
description: The ability to pass through devices provides the capability to offload tasks from the CPU to the device itself.
rationale: Restricting passthrough to approved devicesreduces the risk of unauthorized or unintended data sharing/transmission introduced by allowing GPU and USB connectivity.
audit: workloads and verify the host devices List the host devices available to virtualization returned are necessary for workload execution. cnv - openshift n - hyperconverged - kubevirt hyperconverged get $ oc -ojsonpath='{.spec.permittedHostDevices}'
remediation: Enforcing limitations to the passthroughof approved devices protects against the introduction of a malicious or unknown device to the system. The system administrator can remove the unwanted devices by patching the HCO object. Internal Only - General { "mediatedDevices": [ { "mdevNameSelector": "DEVICE_A", "resourceName": "vendor.com/DEVICE_A" } ], "pciHostDevices": [ { "externalResourceProvider": true, "pciDeviceSelector": "XXX:YYY", "resourceName": "vendor.com/DEVICE_B" }, { "pciDeviceSelector": "NNN:MMM", "resourceName": "vendor.com/DEVICE_C" } ], "usbHostDevices": [ { "resourceName": "kubevirt.io/storage", "selectors": [ { "product": "0001", "vendor": "46f4" } ] } ] If the first PCI device (the DEVICE_A at index 0, in bold) should not be permitted to be passed through to a VM, here’s how to remove it: $ cnv - n openshift - hyperconverged - oc patch hyperconverged kubevirt --type='json' -p='[ {"op": "remove", "path": "/spec/permittedHostDevices/pciHostDevices/0"}, ]' To remove all permitted devices, use the following: cnv - n openshift - hyperconverged - $ oc patch hyperconverged kubevirt --type='json' -p='[ {"op": "remove", "path": "/spec/permittedHostDevices"}, ]'
impact: Unauthorized devices will not be passed through,and therefore not be available to workloads.
justification: The verification of device passthrough configurations requires human judgment to ensure compliance with organizational policies, which cannot be fully automated. While the retrieval of permitted devices can be scripted, the assessment of whether these devices are necessary and approved involves manual inspection and decision-making. The IBM Cloud APIs and CLI do not directly manage OpenShift Virtualization configurations, which are handled through the OpenShift CLI.
manual_summary: 1. **Access OpenShift Cluster:** Use SSH to connect to a control node in the OpenShift cluster where the Virtualization is hosted. 2. **List Permitted Devices:** Execute the command to list the permitted host devices: ```bash oc -n openshift-hyperconverged get hyperconverged kubevirt -o jsonpath='{.spec.permittedHostDevices}' ``` This command will return the currently configured passthrough devices. 3. **Verify Device List:** Check the output of the command. You should see a list of `pciHostDevices`, `mediatedDevices`, and `usbHostDevices`. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Ensure that the device lists only include those approved for passthrough, based on organizational policy. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** If unauthorized devices are found in the list, refer to the remediation instructions to update the configuration and remove any unwanted devices.
default_value: By default, OpenShift Virtualization doesn’t configure any pass through devices.The empty result of the command should be . Internal Only - General

title: Restrict update access to the CDI CR
description: The contains sensitive configuration data for the object is not namespaced and CDI Containerized Data Importer.
rationale: Reduce at the minimum who can modify resources. CDI
audit: check who can update the resources: cdi $ cdi update can - who policy adm oc
remediation: Assign cdi access only to approved update objects in the cluster.
impact: Workload owners don’t normally need this feature.
justification: The task of checking who has update access to CDI resources can be automated using the OpenShift CLI (`oc`). The second AI opinion correctly identifies that a script can be created to periodically execute the necessary commands and parse the output for unauthorized access. This aligns with IBM Cloud's capabilities for automation and monitoring, making it feasible to automate this compliance check.
automation_summary: - IBM Cloud Resource/Service: OpenShift Cluster - API/CLI Method: OpenShift CLI (`oc get clusterrolebindings --all-namespaces | grep cdi`) - Properties to Check: Output of the `oc get clusterrolebindings` command to identify users or service accounts with CDI update permissions. - Pass Criteria: Only approved user accounts or service accounts have update permissions. - Fail Criteria: Any unauthorized users or service accounts have update permissions on CDI resources.
default_value: This permission is not granted by default.

title: nonRoot
description: Root is a default account with elevated privileges, including full administrative access to security related functions. The "nonRoot" feature gate in - both security and non OpenShift environments ensures virtual machine access is restricted to resources and functionality required to operate.
rationale: Unauthorized access to a root account withoutrestrictions implemented by the nonRoot feature introduces the risk of unintended or unauthorized access to privilege elevation and the ability to perform administrative tasks. This feature is a foundational mechanism in implementing role and attribute based access controls.
audit: Use the following command to list for hyper converged resources: settings nonRoot $ oc cnv - openshift n - hyperconverged - kubevirt hyperconverged get -ojsonpath='{.spec.featureGates.nonRoot}' Verify the output is true . nonRoot is deprecated in Openshift Virtualization from 4.18, and the secure default is enforced.
remediation: Enable True and set its value to nonRoot . $ oc patch hyperconverged kubevirt-hyperconverged cnv - n openshift - --type='json' -p='[ {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/featureGates/nonRoot", "value": true }, ]'
impact: Disabling root introduces the need to implementalternate identification and authentication methods to access administrative functions.
justification: The control can be effectively verified using the OpenShift CLI, which is a suitable tool for this task given the nature of the control and the environment it applies to. Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated, and the OpenShift CLI command provided is technically accurate for checking the 'nonRoot' feature gate status. While the second opinion suggests considering IBM Cloud-specific tools, the OpenShift CLI is already well-integrated with IBM Cloud's Kubernetes services, making it an appropriate choice for automation.
automation_summary: - IBM Cloud Resource/Service: OpenShift on IBM Cloud - API/CLI Method: OpenShift CLI command `oc get hyperconverged kubevirt-hyperconverged -n openshift -o jsonpath='{.spec.featureGates.nonRoot}'` - Properties to Check: `.spec.featureGates.nonRoot` - Pass Criteria: The output must be `true` - Fail Criteria: The output is anything other than `true`
default_value: By default, KubeVirt workloads run . From false in nonRoot mode and it sets to 4.18, this field will be ignored, and always be enabled. Internal Only - General

title: Disable nested virtualization
description: Enabling nested virtualization in OpenShift Virtualization allows virtual machines to run within a hypervisor that is itself running on top of an existing operating system. This nested virtualization, enables the VMs to access the full range of setup, known asvirtualization features provided by the underlying host, including access to the virtualization stack. In this case, Openshift Virtualization uses nested virtualization to provide its VMs with access to the entire virtualization stack, allowing them to take advantage of advanced virtualization capabilities such as live migration, resource pooling, and cloning. Nest virtualization is required if the cluster admin expects users to run Windows guest and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) 2.
rationale: While nested virtualization has safeguards in place to prevent malicious activity, disabling it undeniably reduces the attack surface.
audit: To check the state of nested virtualization on nodes running on Intel CPUs: $ /sys/module/kvm_intel/parameters/nested cat Disabled is denoted by If you’re using AMD CPUs, . 1 and enable is denoted with , 0 . kvm_amd with kvm_intel replace
remediation: Add the appropriate kernel arguments to the MachineConfigPools of the workers nodes and reboot them. See https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.18/html/installation - kargs_installing - config - special - customizing#installation - _configuration/installing customizing Internal Only - General
impact: Nested virtualization is required for special use-cases when cluster-admins expect to use virtualization inside the guests i.e. if the VMs in the cluster may need virtualization, for WSL2 or other purposes. Note:If nested virtualization is disabled, Openshift Virtualization VMs will not be able to use hardware virtualization to start their own VMs. Windows guests will also be unable to use WSL2.
justification: The task involves checking nested virtualization settings on OpenShift worker nodes, which requires accessing system-level configurations not directly exposed through IBM Cloud APIs or CLI tools. Both AI opinions acknowledge the necessity of SSH access to execute commands on the nodes, indicating that automation via IBM Cloud's existing tools is not feasible without custom scripting. Therefore, a manual approach is required to ensure compliance.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the OpenShift Console:** - Log in to your OpenShift Console (web interface) with cluster administrator credentials. 2. **SSH into the Worker Nodes:** - Use `ssh` to connect to each worker node in the OpenShift cluster. 3. **Check the Nested Virtualization Setting:** - For Intel CPUs, run: ```bash cat /sys/module/kvm_intel/parameters/nested ``` - For AMD CPUs, run: ```bash cat /sys/module/kvm_amd/parameters/nested ``` 4. **Verify the Output:** - Ensure the output is `0`, indicating nested virtualization is disabled. 5. **Document the Findings:** - Record whether nested virtualization is enabled or disabled for each node.
default_value: OpenShift does not enable nested virtualization by default.

manual_summary: 1. **Access the OpenShift Command Line Interface (CLI):** - Connect to your worker nodes in the OpenShift cluster through SSH. 2. **Check Kernel Command Line:** - Execute the following command to check the kernel command line: ``` cat /proc/cmdline ``` 3. **Verify the Presence of the Option:** - Look for the string `schedstats=enable` in the output of the previous command. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The output should include `schedstats=enable`.
